episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] B.C. premier’s office says DRIPA changes will not be introduced this spring after all and [100 Mile Free Press] Cancer patient donates saddle sale to 100 Mile House oncology department, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can rural Cariboo communities build lasting digital storytelling capacity that preserves cultural memory while maintaining sovereignty over their own narratives? Topics include: Digitization infrastructure vs. storytelling capacity building, Cultural sovereignty and governance in digital archiving, Interpretive labor and the role of elders in meaning-making, Technology accessibility for rural communities, Institutional partnerships and power dynamics in cultural preservation.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Partners for Literacy

discussion.central_question: How can rural Cariboo communities build lasting digital storytelling capacity that preserves cultural memory while maintaining sovereignty over their own narratives?
discussion.riley_position: The real opportunity lies not in digitizing existing materials but in building new storytelling capacity through accessible technology and clear governance structures that communities can control themselves.
riley_key_evidence: Mukurtu CMS has sustained community archive projects for 15+ years through granular access controls rather than flashy technology
Hardware costs for digitization and storage have dropped dramatically, making consumer-grade equipment viable for community organizations
AI tools can assist with metadata (facial recognition, transcription, tagging) but cannot generate the interpretive meaning that lived experience provides
discussion.casey_position: The critical missing layer is the interpretive work—the contextualization of digital materials—which requires pairing digitization with structured oral history collection and depends on time-sensitive knowledge from elders and community knowledge-keepers.
casey_key_evidence: Digital files without narrative context are not meaningful archives; a folder of 10,000 JPEGs requires someone who lived the experience to provide meaning
Indigenous-led tech projects are systematically under-resourced compared to venture-funded urban AI tools, creating asymmetry in whose stories get told
DRIPA delay leaves Indigenous communities in a governance vacuum while physical cultural materials continue to deteriorate
discussion.resolution: Casey and Riley converged on a shared framework: community-scale memory sprints pairing digitization with recorded narration, governed locally without waiting for provincial frameworks, with equal partnership between cultural offices and libraries rather than top-down institutional models.
topics_covered: Digitization infrastructure vs. storytelling capacity building
Cultural sovereignty and governance in digital archiving
Interpretive labor and the role of elders in meaning-making
Institutional partnerships and power dynamics in cultural preservation
